title: The Hobbit: Adventure Board Game (1995) (RetroLook)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xMbY5fkmK0A

Daniel (@dungeondive) takes a RetroLook at The Hobbit: Adventure Board Game (ICE, ~1994–95), opening with praise for its gorgeous Angus McBride cover and lovely mounted map. It's a Talisman-style race: hobbits roam Middle-earth gathering gear, spells and Talismans, fighting monsters to grow strong enough to journey north and battle the dragon for its hoard. Light and quick (rules in four pages), it's no solo game but plays fine solo the way he plays Talisman or Runebound.

He tours its eight card decks — peril, danger, rest, adventure, village goods, riddles, magic — highlighting the clever riddle battles (you answer by holding a card bearing the answer) and the quirky three-d3 combat with a Yahtzee-like re-roll. His verdict: a decent entry-level adventure game and a good, still-affordable start to a vintage collection, held back by two fixable flaws — interchangeable hobbits with no characters or powers, and a flat endgame (one blurry dragon, no card, no variety). He closes with a warm digression on his childhood Rankin/Bass-illustrated copy of The Hobbit, and his preference for it over Lord of the Rings.

----

Daniel's main wish for The Hobbit board game is distinct, characterful hobbits instead of identical pawns. How much does having a hero you can latch onto — a name, a power, a personality — matter to your enjoyment?
